meta.title: Yeonggeumjeong Sunrise Pavilion | Sokcho, Gangwon-do, Korea
meta.description: The complete guide to Yeonggeumjeong Sunrise Pavilion (영금정 해돋이정자): a traditional wooden pavilion built on seaside rocks above the East Sea in Sokcho, Gangwon-do. Opening hours, how to get there, parking & accessibility, visitor reviews and photo spots — all in one place.

nonprofitBanner.text: This is an independent, non-profit guide compiled by travel enthusiasts to provide the most objective information about visiting Yeonggeumjeong. It is free, and we are affiliated with no organization.

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.text: So that every traveler who visits Sokcho does not miss the stunning sunrise over the East Sea, we have gathered scattered official information into this guide — purely to give you the most authentic viewing and cultural experience.

hero.title: Yeonggeumjeong Sunrise Pavilion
hero.subtitle: A traditional wooden pavilion perched on coastal rocks above the East Sea in Sokcho, Gangwon-do. Open 24 hours and free of charge, it is the top spot in Sokcho to watch the sunrise over the sea and one of the 'Sokcho Eight Views' — climb the pavilion at dawn and watch the sun rise from the horizon.

quickFactsDashboard.title: Yeonggeumjeong at a Glance
quickFactsDashboard.subtitle: Key facts gathered in one place for quick reading and fast decisions.

items.label: Highlight
items.note: The pavilion juts out on rocks facing due east — Sokcho's best sunrise viewpoint.

items.label: Hours
items.note: No gate, no closing time; arrive any time. Dawn is quietest and most photogenic.

items.label: Admission
items.note: A public coastal site with no ticket and no reservation needed.

hiddenRules.title: Safety Tips for the Pavilion & Shore
hiddenRules.subtitle: Yeonggeumjeong sits on rocks jutting into the sea where conditions change fast. Know these in advance for a safe sunrise and a smooth visit.

highlights.label: Waves & spray
highlights.note: The eastern rocks are constantly hit by waves; at high tide or in strong wind the spray can cover the path. Do not cross rails to get close.

highlights.label: Slippery surfaces
highlights.note: Dew, sea mist and rain make stone steps slippery at dawn — wear grippy shoes and hold the rails.

highlights.label: Night & winter
highlights.note: Lighting around the pavilion is limited at night; in winter the sea wind is strong and wood/stone can freeze — dress warm and watch your step.

panels.q: Are there limits on climbing the pavilion or approaching the rocks?
panels.a: The pavilion itself is open with no gate, but the surrounding rocks are exposed to the sea without full railings. Do not climb over the outer rails or step down onto wave-washed rocks, especially at high tide or in rough seas.
items: Watch the sunrise from inside the pavilion and the paved walkway; stay off the slippery rocks within wave reach.
Keep children and seniors accompanied and away from unrailed edges.

panels.q: Why be extra careful at night and in winter?
panels.a: Sokcho winters bring strong sea winds and low temperatures; wood and stone steps can frost over, and the site is dimly lit at night. For a sunrise plan, arrive 30 minutes early with a headlamp or phone light and walk slowly.

panels.q: Can I bring food, drink alcohol or camp?
panels.a: This is a public coastal site — please avoid eating, drinking alcohol or overnight camping inside the wooden pavilion, and keep the area clean. Take your trash with you or use the designated bins.

complianceGuide.title: Yeonggeumjeong · Visitor & Shore Etiquette
complianceGuide.subtitle: This is both a local seaside retreat and a public coast we must protect together. Following these rules is basic respect for nature and others.

rules.title: Do not climb rails or down the rocks
rules.text: The pavilion juts over East Sea rocks; the outside is mostly unguarded, slippery reef. Big waves can sweep over instantly. Stay on the paved path and in the pavilion.
items: Stop immediately at signs reading 'Danger' or 'Watch for falling into the sea'.
For photos, never step over the rail or onto wet rocks for a better angle.

rules.title: Take your trash with you
rules.text: Public bins along the coast are limited and sea wind scatters litter. Bring a small bag and take everything away when you leave.

rules.title: Keep quiet at dawn
rules.text: Many visitors come specifically to wait for the sunrise in silence. Before and around sunrise, please lower your voice and avoid loud music.

rules.title: No smoking or open flame in the pavilion
rules.text: The pavilion is wooden and exposed to sea wind — smoke only in designated areas and never use open flame, to prevent fire and burns.

higashiyamaRoute.title: Sokcho Half-Day 'Sunrise Chase' Route
higashiyamaRoute.subtitle: Make Yeonggeumjeong your first stop at dawn, then tour Sokcho's beaches, culture and food — all in a relaxed half day.

steps.title: [Start] Arrive at Yeonggeumjeong for sunrise
steps.text: Arrive 30 minutes early and wait for the sun to rise from the East Sea; at low tide stroll the paved reef shore but stay off the wave zone.
tips: Summer sunrise ~05:00–05:30, winter ~07:30 — check the day's time before you go.

steps.title: [Stop 2] Seaside walk at Sokcho Beach
steps.text: Follow the wooden boardwalk from Sokcho Beach toward the city — sea breeze and sand make the most pleasant morning stretch.

steps.title: [Stop 3] Abai Village & Hamgyong cuisine
steps.text: Cross the footbridge or walk to Abai Village for local Hamgyong-style squid and potato dishes.

steps.title: [Stop 4] Sokcho Expo Tower & Marine Cable Car
steps.text: Return downtown to climb the Expo Tower or ride the marine cable car for a high vantage over Yeonggeumjeong and the coast.

steps.title: [End] Sunset or dinner at Cheongcho Lake
steps.text: In the evening head to Cheongcho Lake's lakeside path for a sea-meets-lake sunset, then dine nearby.
tips: Treat 'a clean sunrise shot' as a bonus; on bad weather switch to walks and food.

higashiyamaRoute.disclaimer: This is a typical pace; season, weather and traffic affect walking speed — adjust flexibly to on-site conditions.

intro.title: Getting to Know Yeonggeumjeong
intro.description: Yeonggeumjeong stands on a reef jutting into the East Sea on the east side of Sokcho, Gangwon-do. Legend says the rocks here once emitted a clear, zither-like sound, giving the name 'Yeonggeum' (spirit zither); the 'jeong' is the traditional wooden viewing pavilion built by the sea. Long listed among the 'Sokcho Eight Views', it is the landmark where citizens and travelers greet the sunrise and listen to the tide.

visitGuide.title: Visitor Guide
visitGuide.description: Yeonggeumjeong is open 24 hours and free, with no gate and no ticket. Make dawn viewing your main plan and combine it with nearby Sokcho Beach, Abai Village and the Expo Tower. The pavilion sits on open coast with minimal facilities — do your supplies in the city.
items: Free and open all day; dawn is best for few crowds and beautiful light.
Best angles are inside the pavilion facing the sea and the extended boardwalk; at low tide the reef lines photograph best.
On coastal rocks, reachable from Sokcho city/beach on foot, by bus or taxi in about 10–15 minutes.
Nearest restrooms and food are around Sokcho Beach and the bus terminal (~10–15 min walk) — stock up beforehand.

alsoKnownAs.title: Highlights
items: A representative East Sea sunrise spot, facing due east
One of the 'Sokcho Eight Views' and Sokcho's most recognizable coastal landmark
Named 'Yeonggeum' from the legend of rocks that sound like a zither
Free and open 24 hours — an accessible place to meet the East Sea and dawn

transportSection.title: Transport & Parking Guide
transportSection.subtitle: A structured guide to reaching Yeonggeumjeong, including transfers, walking/cycling options, parking, and EV charging notes.
transportSection.overviewText: Sokcho lies on the northeast coast of Gangwon-do. The easiest external link is an express/intercity bus from Seoul straight to the Sokcho Express Bus Terminal (~2h40); you can also take a train toward Gangneung and transfer. In the city, Yeonggeumjeong sits on the coast north of Dongmyeong Port — typically ~10–15 minutes by taxi from the terminal, or ~15–20 minutes on foot from Sokcho Lighthouse Beach.
transportSection.overviewText2: Yeonggeumjeong is built on rocks jutting into the East Sea, surrounded by open coast with no walls or gates. Plan transport, parking and walking together to save time and avoid on-site confusion.
arrivalItems: Yeonggeumjeong is on Sokcho's east coast north of Dongmyeong Port; from the terminal it's typically ~10–15 minutes by vehicle, and on foot it's most practical from nearby coastal areas.
There is no large dedicated lot — use the Sokcho Beach public parking or nearby paid lots, then walk.
In summer (esp. Jul–Aug) and on holidays the coast road jams badly; strongly prefer transit or off-peak.

modes.label: Plane (Yangyang / Seoul)
modes.title: From Yangyang Int'l or Seoul airports
modes.description: Common for overseas and domestic long-haul; Yangyang is closer but has limited flights, Seoul has frequent ones.
details: Yangyang Int'l (YNY): ~40 km from Sokcho, a few domestic/international charters; then airport bus or taxi ~40–50 min to Sokcho.
Seoul Incheon (ICN)/Gimpo (GMP): take airport rail or bus into Seoul, then transfer to the Sokcho-bound intercity bus (below), ~3–4h total.
At Sokcho terminal, transfer to a city bus or taxi to Yeonggeumjeong (below).

steps.t: Fly to Yangyang (YNY) or first to a Seoul airport.
From Yangyang take the airport bus/taxi; from Seoul transfer to the Sokcho intercity bus.
At Sokcho terminal transfer to a city bus or taxi, ~10–15 min to Yeonggeumjeong.

modes.label: Train (KTX / ITX)
modes.title: Via Gangneung transfer
modes.description: Sokcho has no direct KTX station yet; go to Gangneung or Jinbu first, then transfer to a bus.
details: From Seoul Cheongnyangni take ITX-Chuncheon or KTX-EUM to Gangneung (~2h), then an intercity bus to Sokcho (~40–60 min).
You can also train to Jinbu (Odaesan) and transfer. Gangneung is also the 2018 PyeongChang gateway, with easy transfers.
Use a T-money/transit card — buses and city transit accept tap payment, less queuing.

steps.t: Take ITX/KTX from Seoul to Gangneung Station.
At Gangneung bus terminals transfer to a Sokcho-bound bus.
At Sokcho terminal take a city bus or taxi ~10–15 min to Yeonggeumjeong.

modes.label: Public transit (intercity / city bus)
modes.title: Seoul direct to Sokcho Express Bus
modes.description: Easiest for most travelers; several Seoul hubs reach Sokcho directly.
details: Seoul Express Bus Terminal (Gangnam) or Dong-Seoul Terminal run frequent intercity buses to 'Sokcho Express Bus Terminal', ~2h40.
In Sokcho: from the terminal take a city bus toward 'Sokcho Beach / Expo' and get off near Yeonggeumjeong, then walk a few minutes.
City buses take T-money; schedules vary by season — check live arrival in a maps app.

steps.t: Take an intercity bus from Seoul straight to Sokcho Express Bus Terminal.
Transfer to a city bus toward the beach/Expo direction.
Get off near Yeonggeumjeong and walk the coast ~5–10 min to the pavilion.

modes.label: Car (parking / charging)
modes.title: Coastal road · nearby parking
modes.description: Good when traveling with kids/elders, lots of luggage, or touring the East Coast; no big dedicated lot at the pavilion.
details: Set destination to 'Yeonggeumjeong (영금정)' or '43 Yeonggeumjeong-ro, Sokcho'.
Small parking strips near the pavilion and the Sokcho Beach public lot fill fast on weekends/peak — arrive early or park farther and walk.
Some public lots have EV chargers; coastal winds are strong, mind crosswinds when parking.

steps.t: Park in a public/paid lot and walk to the pavilion.
Avoid the 09:00–16:00 peak to save parking time.

modes.label: Taxi / ride-hail
modes.title: Door-to-door
modes.description: Easiest with luggage, kids/elders, or early/late arrival.
details: Taxi from Sokcho Express Bus Terminal to Yeonggeumjeong ~10 min, about KRW 6,000–9,000 (meter basis).
From Sokcho Beach area just a few minutes; ask to drop you at the roadside before the uphill.
Taxis also queue in peak/rainy days — leave buffer and book via a local app (e.g., Kakao T).

steps.t: Book via Kakao T at the terminal or hotel.
Get off near the pavilion and walk to the coast.

modes.label: Bike / walk
modes.title: East Coast bike path & shore stroll
modes.description: The most relaxing way to feel the East Sea.
details: Sokcho's coastal bike path runs from Sokcho Beach to the Yeonggeumjeong area; sea breeze and reefs accompany you.
Paved paths connect the pavilion with the beach and Cheongcho Lake; park bikes at the rack outside, no riding on the boardwalk.
About 15–20 min walk from the beach, with rest points and sea-view decks along the way.

steps.t: Rent a public bike at the beach.
Ride the East Coast bike path toward Yeonggeumjeong.
Lock at the rack outside and walk in to view.

modes.label: Walk (neighborhood roam)
modes.title: From the beach to the pavilion
modes.description: If you are already on Sokcho's coast, walking is the most natural way.
details: From Sokcho Beach, follow the wooden boardwalk east ~15–20 min to Yeonggeumjeong.
You can also walk from Abai Village along the coast, with food and sea-view stops.
Coastal stone and boardwalk have slight grades — wear comfy shoes and watch children.

parking.title: Parking & Charging (driver reference)
parking.note: Yeonggeumjeong has no large dedicated lot. Below are the nearest main parking and charging options; rates and availability vary by season/time — see on-site signs.

options.name: Sokcho Beach public parking
options.price: Hourly/daily; public rates lower, fills in peak

options.name: Small parking strips by pavilion
options.price: Roadside/small lot, few spaces, easier off-peak

options.name: Terminal-area parking
options.price: Large facility, good for overnight/full day

parking.disclaimer: Coastal roads are narrow — do not block bus/fire lanes for long. EV chargers are mostly at the beach public lot and some municipal lots; none dedicated at the pavilion. Rates/limits may change — see posted signs.

driverFaq.a: No large dedicated lot, but the Sokcho Beach public lot and small strips by the pavilion are a few minutes' walk. They fill fast on weekends/peak — arrive early or park farther and walk.
Small strips by the pavilion are ~100–300 m, the closest; the beach public lot is ~600–900 m with more spaces but tight in peak.
Very little. The coastal road is narrow — do not park roadside for long; use proper lots and transit.
Unless you need parking, better not. The coast jams 09:00–16:00; walking or transit is smoother. If driving, park nearby and walk in.
Strongly. Intercity bus from Seoul to Sokcho terminal, then city bus/taxi ~10–15 min. Address: 43 Yeonggeumjeong-ro, Dongmyeong-dong, Sokcho-si, Gangwon-do.
Best by transit: Seoul intercity bus to Sokcho terminal, then bus/taxi; or drive and park nearby then walk. Address: 43 Yeonggeumjeong-ro, Sokcho-si, Gangwon-do.

localTips: Dawn (30 min before sunrise) has the fewest people and best light — prime for viewing and photos.
At low tide the reef lines and tide pools photograph best; at high tide/big waves step back to the paved path for safety.
Link Yeonggeumjeong with Sokcho Beach, Abai Village, Expo Tower and Cheongcho Lake into a half-day coast line.

bestTime.title: Best time to arrive
bestTime.text: Yeonggeumjeong is visitable any time, but dawn is typically the best window to observe the pavilion silhouette, sea spray, and the East Sea horizon together. If the morning is cloudy or windy, shift the focus to coastal walks and nearby cultural stops.

officialManagement.title: About the Pavilion
officialManagement.text: Yeonggeumjeong is a traditional wooden viewing pavilion on coastal rocks on the east side of Sokcho, Gangwon-do, managed by Sokcho City as a public coastal site. Long listed among the 'Sokcho Eight Views', it is an open place for citizens to relax and for visitors to watch the East Sea sunrise and hear the tide. As a public coastal landmark, it offers citizens and visitors a space to connect with nature and learn about Joseon-era pavilion culture and East Sea geography.

humanities.title: History & On-site Inscriptions
humanities.subtitle: A structured reading of Yeonggeumjeong through place-name records, rebuilding history, and the three on-site plaques.

sections.title: Name origin: the zither on the rocks and Seontodo
sections.content: The name Yeonggeumjeong comes from a long-told legend — the rocks jutting into the East Sea were said to emit a clear, six-string-zither-like sound under wind and tide, like a 'spirit zither' (Yeonggeum); the 'jeong' is the wooden pavilion later built by the sea. The place name first appeared in the 1926 Yeonse Eupji (延歳邑誌). Joseon-era records (including Kim Se-jun's Daedong Jiji) also call it 'Seontodo' (仙桃台): legend says fairies descended nightly to bathe and play the zither, giving the name; 'Yeonggeum' captures the sea sounding like a fairy's instrument, stirring tender feeling.

sections.title: Damage under Japanese rule and reconstruction
sections.content: The area was once superb scenery, but most of the rocks were destroyed when Sokcho Port was built during the Japanese colonial period. Today's wooden pavilion is not a Joseon relic: the current sunrise pavilion was funded by donations from Dongmyeong-dong residents, raised by the Dongmyeong-dong Development Promotion Committee (formerly a residents' autonomous committee), and built in 1997; the footbridge 'Dongmyeonghaegyo' (East-Bright Sea Bridge) connecting the pavilion was built by Sokcho City in 1998. After long wear, it was renovated and rebuilt, and in September 2017 the mayor of Sokcho erected a monument — it remains a top Korean tourist spot to enjoy the East Sea sunrise and citizens' memories.

sections.title: Among the 'Sokcho Eight Views'
sections.content: In Sokcho's long-running 'Eight Views', the 'sunrise over the sea' at Yeonggeumjeong has always held a place. The first sunlight from the East Sea on the rocks and wooden pavilion is among Sokcho's most recognizable and cherished scenes.

sections.title: Wood and rock in harmony
sections.content: The pavilion rests on rocks with wooden columns, wide eaves and open sides — light yet never stealing the sea view. The structure follows sea wind and terrain, plain and practical, with no ornate painting, forming a harmonious East Coast picture with waves and reefs. The lacquered wooden placard '靈琴亭' (Yeonggeumjeong), read right-to-left in the traditional style, is the core landmark visitors photograph.

sections.title: A citizens' coastal living room
sections.content: Today Yeonggeumjeong has shifted from a scholars' retreat to a public coastal site anyone can reach. Open 24 hours and free, with no gate, it draws sunrise chasers at dawn and strollers at dusk — a node where Sokcho's daily life and tourism meet. A wooden placard by the pavilion records the 1997 donors who funded the pavilion and bridge — a rare civilian merit monument.

sections.title: A green landmark of Sokcho's East Coast
sections.content: From the pavilion the East Sea stretches wide; looking back, Sokcho's city and hills. It is both a coordinate for watching the sunrise and a cultural link to the beach, Abai Village and Cheongcho Lake — a spiritual corner of Sokcho's coast.

didYouKnow.title: Did you know?
didYouKnow.text: The most charming 'acoustic legend' of Yeonggeumjeong remains unexplained: waves hitting certain rocks really can sound like strings. Though unsettled, this weave of nature and imagination is exactly why it was listed among the 'Sokcho Eight Views' and recited for generations.

sitePlaques.title: Reading the Three On-site Plaques
sitePlaques.subtitle: When you visit, three official plaques and wooden boards stand by the pavilion — the most authoritative first-hand source on its history. We translate and explain each one.
sitePlaques.intro: The following readings are based on on-site photos of the monument, bilingual signboard and wooden placards erected by Sokcho City.

items.title: Black monument (erected by Sokcho City, 2017)
items.koTitle: 영금정(靈琴亭) 해돋이 정자(亭子)와 동명해교(東明海橋)
items.koTitleZh: Yeonggeumjeong Sunrise Pavilion and Dongmyeonghaegyo Bridge
items.body: The inscription records: the famous sunrise spot Yeonggeumjeong Sunrise Pavilion was built in 1997 with funds donated voluntarily by Dongmyeong-dong residents, raised by the Dongmyeong-dong Development Promotion Committee (formerly a residents' autonomous committee); the footbridge Dongmyeonghaegyo connecting the pavilion was built by Sokcho City in 1998. Long a known tourist spot, after aging facilities were renovated and rebuilt, it remains a top Korean destination to enjoy the East Sea sunrise and citizens' cherished memories. Signed: September 2017, Mayor of Sokcho.

items.title: Name-origin signboard (Korean–English)
items.body: Top Korean text: Yeonggeumjeong Rock lies on coastal rocks east of the Dongmyeong-dong lighthouse; the name was first recorded in the 1926 Yeonse Eupji. 'Yeonggeum' means the waves striking the rocks make a clear, six-string-zither-like sound, hence the name. Most rocks were destroyed when Sokcho Port was built during the Japanese colonial period. Per old Joseon records (Kim Se-jun's Daedong Jiji), it was also called 'Seontodo' (仙桃台); legend says fairies descended nightly to bathe and play the zither. The lower half gives an English Introduction; the footer reads SOKCHO 束草.

items.title: Wooden plaque and donor list
items.koTitle: 靈琴亭 · 湖亭 (plaque) / 출자자 명부 (merit board)
items.body: At top, a black-lacquered gold-lettered wooden plaque '靈琴亭' (Yeonggeumjeong), read right-to-left in the traditional style, with small side characters '湖亭' (Hojeong). A brown sign marks: △ the original plaque of the earliest-built Yeonggeumjeong Sunrise Pavilion, ▽ the donor list for the pavilion's construction funds. The long bottom wooden board densely records the 1997 donors — local residents who funded the pavilion and bridge, with names, amounts and addresses — a civilian merit monument.

gallery.title: Photo Gallery
gallery.subtitle: From sea sunrise, waves on rocks, pavilion silhouettes to four-season coast — see the visual beauty of Yeonggeumjeong.

reviews.title: Visitor Reviews
reviews.declaration: Visitor feedback is available on Google Maps (external link).
reviews.featuredSubtitle: A few short lines that capture the spirit of Yeonggeumjeong.

featured.text: Watching the sunrise from the pavilion, the moment the horizon lit up little by little was truly breathtaking — worth the trip.
Walking the reef at low tide, the sound of waves with the wooden pavilion is Sokcho's most soothing morning.
Free and open all day; just drop by for the sea with kids — easy and relaxed.

items.name: Minjun
items.text: Arrived before sunrise and waited; the moment the sun rose from the East Sea the whole place went quiet — strongly recommend dawn, few people and great light.

items.name: Seoyeon
items.text: At low tide the reef lines are beautiful and photograph well. The pavilion is small but faces the sea, very open and comfortable.

items.name: Jihun
items.text: Worth it as a free coastal site; winter wind is strong so dress warm. For a clean sunrise shot come early.

items.name: Ha-eun
items.text: About 15 min walk from the beach, nice sea views along the way, easy with kids. Food nearby is convenient.

mapSection.subtitle: 43 Yeonggeumjeong-ro, Dongmyeong-dong, Sokcho-si, Gangwon-do, Korea (Plus Code: 6J62+RR)

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Practical info about Yeonggeumjeong's facilities, history and visit planning.

items.label: Parking
items.value: Beach public lot / small strips by pavilion

items.label: Accessibility
items.value: Paved path accessible; reef needs assist

items.q: Is there parking, and what are the rates?
items.a: Yeonggeumjeong has no large dedicated lot, but the Sokcho Beach public lot and small strips by the pavilion are a few minutes' walk. Public lots are mostly hourly/daily and cheaper; they fill fast on weekends/peak, so arrive early or prefer transit.

items.q: Are wheelchairs or strollers allowed in the area?
items.a: Paved paths and boardwalk reach near the pavilion and are usable with wheelchairs/strollers; but the reef section is natural stone steps, uneven and not fully railed — go with assistance and avoid wet/wave zones.

items.q: Are there restrooms or food at the site?
items.a: The pavilion is open coast with no restroom or food; nearest public toilets and dining are around Sokcho Beach and the bus terminal (~10–15 min walk) — stock up before you go.

items.q: Why is it called 'Yeonggeum', and does it relate to the rocks?
items.a: Legend says the rocks jutting into the East Sea emitted a clear, zither-like sound under wind and tide, like a 'spirit zither' (Yeonggeum); the pavilion was later built by the sea. The legend made the rocks and waves Sokcho's most poetic symbol.

items.q: What is special about its landform and architecture?
items.a: The pavilion rests on rocks with open sides and wide eaves — plain and practical, harmonizing with waves and reef. Sited facing due east, it has been a prime sunrise spot since ancient times and is one of the 'Sokcho Eight Views'.
Yeonggeumjeong is a public coastal site, open 24 hours and free, with no gate, no ticket and no reservation — visit anytime.

items.q: How long does a visit take?
items.a: A typical visit is about 30 min to 1 hour: view the sea from the pavilion, stroll the boardwalk and reef, or sit for a sunrise. Allow half a day if adding Sokcho Beach, Abai Village and the Expo Tower.

items.q: Can I still go in bad weather?
items.a: Yes — it is open coast, visitable in any weather. But rocks are slippery, winter ice forms, and big waves can cover the path; wear grippy shoes, stay off unrailed edges, and check the day's sea and sunrise forecast.

items.q: After visiting, what other attractions are worth it?
items.a: From the pavilion you can link Sokcho Beach, Abai Village, the Expo Tower and marine cable car, and Cheongcho Lake; farther out are Seoraksan National Park and Yangyang — forming a complete East Coast line.

attractions.name: Sokcho Expo Tower / Marine Cable Car

scienceArt.title: Architecture & Legend of Yeonggeumjeong: Wood, Rock and Zither
scienceArt.subtitle: Yeonggeumjeong is more than a sea-view spot — it is public heritage weaving Joseon pavilion culture, seaside siting wisdom and folk legend. Two angles to read it.

scienceMuseum.name: Wood & Rock
scienceMuseum.role: A seaside pavilion built over the sea
scienceMuseum.highlight: The pavilion rests on East Sea rocks with wooden columns, open sides and wide eaves. The structure follows sea wind and terrain — plain and practical, with no ornate painting, yet harmonizing with waves and reefs; a vivid example of the Joseon 'jeongja' type.
points: Siting: faces due east — long a prime spot to welcome the sun and watch the tide.
Structure: wooden columns, eaves and open facade, light yet never stealing the sea view.
Materials: local wood and stone, plain and durable.

artMuseum.name: The Zither Legend
artMuseum.role: A cultural symbol of the Sokcho Eight Views
artMuseum.highlight: The name 'Yeonggeum' comes from the legend of wind and waves sounding like a zither on the rocks. Though its acoustic cause is unsettled, this weave of nature and imagination placed it among the 'Sokcho Eight Views' and made it an East Sea image recited for generations.
points: Legend: rocks sounded like a zither in wind and waves, hence 'Yeonggeum'.
Status: a longstanding 'Sokcho Eight Views' sunrise spot.
Image: a cultural link joining sea, rock, pavilion and citizens' memory.

outdoorMuseum.title: Photo & Shooting Guide: Yeonggeumjeong Spots
outdoorMuseum.subtitle: As Sokcho's most recognizable coastal landmark, a few structured spots and times greatly boost your photos' usefulness and beauty.

items.name: Sunrise · pavilion & horizon
items.background: Arrive 30 min before sunrise and wait for the sun to rise from the East Sea. Frame with the wooden pavilion as foreground and the horizon as extension — Yeonggeumjeong's classic 'welcome the sun' composition.
points: Use the pavilion as foreground, place horizon and sun in the upper third.
Crouch low to layer the eaves and sea for a steadier frame.

items.name: Waves on the rocks
items.background: At low tide walk down the boardwalk to catch waves splashing the rocks. A slow shutter gives silky foam against hard rock.
points: Use long exposure or burst to catch the wave.
Mind slippery footing; shoot from the paved area, not the wave zone.

items.name: Dusk & warm sea
items.location: Pavilion front facing the East Sea
items.background: After sunset the sky warms and the sea reflects orange-gold. The blue moment (20–30 min after sunset) balances sky and sea — the most atmospheric window.
points: Shoot in the blue moment for balanced sky and sea color.
Silhouette the pavilion, leaving a large warm sea.

items.name: Boardwalk & coastline
items.background: Looking back from the beach direction, include Yeonggeumjeong in a wider coastline, with the boardwalk as a leading line — good for a closing wide shot.
points: Use the boardwalk as a leading line toward the pavilion.
Morning mist gives best layers; small aperture for full panorama.

education.title: Why is there a Yeonggeumjeong by the East Sea? (Cultural Meaning)
education.subtitle: A small wooden pavilion by the sea, yet it crossed eras to become Sokcho's visual symbol. Behind it are layered narratives of legend, coast and city memory.

points.title: Origin of legend and coast
points.text: Legend says the rocks sounded like a zither in wind and waves, giving the name 'Yeonggeum'. As a seaside retreat, the pavilion long guarded this transition between mountain and sea.

points.title: A scholars' seaside gathering
points.text: As a Joseon-era 'jeongja', it was where scholars welcomed the sun, watched the tide and composed verse. Thus it became a rare coastal public space that survives and lives with citizens.

points.title: A cultural link across time
points.text: The same pavilion connects the legend of the Eight Views, the landform of the East Sea, and today's beach, Abai Village and Cheongcho Lake. It reminds us: great urban landmarks are often where history and reality coexist.

audience.title: Who Finds Resonance Here? (Audience Guide)
audience.subtitle: Different visitors each find their best 'way in'.

items.resonance: Free, open and safe coastal landmark; kids can see the sea and splash (safely) and meet this seaside wooden pavilion up close.
items.tip: Combine the beach sand with Abai Village snacks — play by the sea then climb the pavilion for an easier pace.

items.resonance: Dawn sunrise and warm dusk sea are Sokcho's most romantic frames, with high shot rates.
items.tip: Take the sea-facing spot inside 30 min before sunrise; shoot blue moment for both lights and sea color.

items.resonance: As an example of the Joseon 'jeongja' type, wood on rock with open sides deserves a close look.
items.tip: Link Yeonggeumjeong with Seoraksan and Gangneung's old pavilions into an 'East Coast pavilion tour'.

items.resonance: No need to go to Jeju or Busan — meet the East Sea sunrise in Sokcho, next to the beach and food; the best start to know Korea's east coast.
items.tip: Intercity bus from Seoul to Sokcho terminal, then bus/taxi ~10–15 min.

lodging.title: Stay Guide: Near the Sea, or Sea View?
lodging.subtitle: Sokcho is a coastal small city where 'sea meets city'. We do not recommend specific hotels, but help you read two lodging patterns to choose what fits you.

options.name: Seaside resort (Sokcho Beach area)
options.role: Closest to sea, sunrise from window
options.highlight: Stay near the beach and walk to Yeonggeumjeong and the shore; open the window at dawn to see the East Sea. Best for travelers whose first goal is 'sea and sunrise'.
points: 15–20 min walk to Yeonggeumjeong — no transport hassle.
Sea-view rooms are great but prices rise sharply in peak season.
Dining and convenience stores nearby, convenient even at night.

options.name: Downtown / near terminal
options.highlight: Stay near the terminal and old town — buses, dining and markets at your door, and day trips to Seoraksan and Yangyang are easier. Best for travelers focused on 'touring the East Coast' with less need for sea view.
points: Bus hub to Seoul and surrounding sights is at your door.
Usually better value and more choices.
To Yeonggeumjeong by bus/taxi ~10–15 min.

lodging.peak: In summer (Jul–Aug) and the winter Seoraksan snow season, rooms tighten severely as vacationers and tourists flood in, and prices rise clearly. Book weeks ahead; if booking near a holiday, widen the search to Gangneung or Yangyang and take a short bus back.
tips: For sunrise: prefer the beach area, walkable to Yeonggeumjeong, no early rush.
For value and transit: choose near the terminal, with bus hub and dining at hand.
Before booking, confirm breakfast, parking and EV chargers (key for drivers).
In peak season and holidays plan ahead to avoid no-room or high prices.
